title: PCs and Workstations: Vying for the 1990's Desktop
author: Peter S. Kastner, John Logan, Thomas Willmott
abstract: This Aberdeen Group workbook analyzes the competitive landscape between Intel-powered PCs and RISC/Unix workstations for enterprise desktop dominance in the 1993-1996 timeframe. It defines workgroup computing requirements, compares platforms across client-server capability, networking, cost, application software, and multiuser compatibility, and evaluates supplier strategies from IBM, Apple, Compaq, Dell, Sun, HP, and operating system vendors. Aberdeen recommends workstations for technical/power users, low-end Unix workstations as NT contingency, Macs for functionality, and PCs for all other users through 1993.
importance_rationale: Provides a structured comparative framework for the pivotal PC-vs-workstation enterprise desktop decision at the inflection point of the client-server era, capturing vendor strategies and buying criteria that shaped 1990s enterprise IT.
relevance_rationale: The framework for evaluating desktop platforms against enterprise workgroup requirements remains applicable; specific product comparisons are historical artifacts of the 1993 market.
prescience_rationale: Aberdeen's prediction that Intel/Windows PCs would win for most commercial users proved correct, as did the forecast that RISC/Unix workstations would remain technical/power-user territory and that OS/2 would fail to displace Windows.
